romania.summary: Pictura contemporană domină opțiunile de achiziție (76%) ale colecționarilor români, în concordanță cu tendințele regionale, urmată de pictura clasică (54%), arată cea de-a doua ediție a studiului Deloitte „Romania Art Report”, realizat în colaborare cu Asociația RAD și cu casa de licitații Artmark. Percepția asupra pieței de artă din România este una predominant orientată spre …

romania.summary: Pesediștii denunță un complot împotriva lor care le face imposibilă prezența la consultările de la Cotroceni. „Primăria a pus otravă de șobolani prin tot Bucureștiul, inclusiv în zona Palatului Cotroceni, exact când noi trebuia să mergem la consultări acolo. Coincidență? Nu cred”, a chițăit Sorin Grindeanu. Liderul PSD spune că atât el, cât și colegii […] The post PSD nu participă la consultările de la Cotroceni, că s-a dat cu otravă de șobolani prin zonă appeared first on Times New Roman .

Nu e tocmai o surpriză că niște polițiști de mâna a doua, cum sunt cei de la Locală, dărâmă o bibliotecă, dar asta nu face gestul lor mai […] The post Cultura, în corzi! romania.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
romania.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.source: Hope and Homes for Children Romania
romania.title: „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj
romania.summary: Hope and Homes for Children, alături de Distribuție Oltenia, parte din Evryo, a derulat campania „Grijă pentru mine”, un proiect dedicat sănătății emoționale aadolescentelor din medii vulnerabile. Inițiativa a ajuns la peste 250 de fete din județul Gorj, inclusiv tinere din sistemul de protecție și din comunități rurale, oferindu-le sprijin pentru a-și înțelege emoțiile, a-șicrește... Articolul „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj apare prima dată în Hope and Homes for Children .

romania.title: De la un proiect Teach for Romania la o inițiativă europeană: cum elevii din Reghin construiesc „Leadership HUB”
romania.summary: O idee pornită din dorința de a dezvolta competențele de leadership ale elevilor s-a transformat, în doar câțiva ani, într-o inițiativă educațională care conectează școli din mai multe țări europene. Povestea proiectului „Leadership HUB” începe cu un proiect finanțat de Teach for Romania și continuă astăzi prin colaborări internaționale în cadrul platformei eTwinning și al […]

medical.title: UK agrees ban on cigarette sales for people born after 2008
medical.summary: Both chambers of Britain's parliament have approved a bill that would ban children aged 17 and under from buying cigarettes during their lifetime.

medical.title: How life-saving HIV therapy has contributed to increased rates of a sexually transmitted disease
medical.summary: A study in Health Economics uncovered an unintended consequence of a major medical breakthrough: while the availability of HIV treatments in the late 1990s dramatically improved survival, they also contributed to a resurgence in syphilis, a sexually transmitted infection.

medical.title: Why electroconvulsive therapy remains contested: Families report memory loss, worse quality of life and limited benefits
medical.summary: Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), which involves passing electricity through the brain under general anesthesia to cause a seizure, usually between six and 12 times, is used to varying degrees around the world for patients with depression and other mental conditions. A study in Psychology and Psychotherapy: Theory, Research and Practice reveals that the experiences of families and friends of individuals undergoing ECT have challenged claims that the therapy is safe and effective.

medical.title: V&A East opens as ‘civic space’ for creativity and community
medical.summary: London’s newest major museum, V&A East, launches in Olympic Park The post V&A East opens as ‘civic space’ for creativity and community appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: Does stress during pregnancy affect offspring's metabolic health?
medical.summary: Physical activity during pregnancy enhances the metabolic health of offspring, but new research in mice indicates that prenatal stress blunts these benefits, at least in male offspring. The findings are published in The FASEB Journal.

medical.title: How children in rural and urban areas experience different mental health struggles
medical.summary: A study in Pediatric Investigation found that rural and urban children and adolescents tend to experience different mental health issues, with those living in the country showing more emotional problems and those in the city demonstrating more behavioral issues.

medical.title: Do drugs that similarly combat a disease have differing effects on quality of life?
medical.summary: A study published in Rheumatology & Autoimmunity challenges the assumption that achieving clinical remission in rheumatoid arthritis is sufficient, showing that patient-reported outcomes vary significantly by drug class even when disease activity is comparable.

medical.title: Study finds prostate cancer overdiagnosis rises sharply with age
medical.summary: Researchers at Queen Mary University of London have found that the likelihood of prostate cancer overdiagnosis—the detection of a cancer that would never have been diagnosed during a patient's lifetime but for PSA screening—is low in younger men but rises substantially with old age.

medical.title: Can sparkling water boost metabolism and help with weight loss?
medical.summary: Sparkling water is often seen as a simple, healthy drink—but could it also help with weight loss? New research suggests it may slightly boost how the body processes blood sugar and energy. However, the effect is very small, meaning it’s no substitute for diet and exercise.

science.title: Cell membranes may store memories after electrical stimulation
science.summary: The science of memories has been pursued and studied since the days of ancient Greece and Aristotle. Today, research conducted by Dima Bolmatov, assistant professor in the Department of Physics & Astronomy at Texas Tech University, is considering how memories are stored on a cellular level.

science.title: Collagen analysis finds wider prey use by Neanderthals and modern humans
science.summary: The Centro Nacional de Investigación sobre la Evolución Humana (CENIEH) has taken part in a study published in the International Journal of Osteoarchaeology that provides new insights into subsistence strategies during the Middle to Upper Paleolithic at the site of Cova Eirós (Cancelo, Triacastela, Lugo). The research applies a collagen-based approach to improve the taxonomic identification of faunal remains.

science.title: Some cancer drugs disrupt taste by changing the cells inside taste buds, study shows
science.summary: Researchers at the University of Colorado Anschutz may have identified why many cancer patients say food suddenly tastes unpleasant during treatment. The study, published today in Development, found that a class of targeted cancer drugs known as tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) can change how taste buds are maintained—reducing the ability to taste sweet foods and altering flavor perception overall. While the study was conducted in animal models, researchers believe similar changes likely occur in humans.

science.title: Rose pangenome maps 55,000 genes, opening new path for breeding
science.summary: Roses are among the most economically significant ornamental plants worldwide, with widespread applications in the cut flowers, garden, and cosmetics industries. Yet fewer than 10% of rose species have contributed to modern cultivated roses. Until recently, available technologies did not allow to fully sequence the Rosa subgenus.

science.title: Researchers develop, validate new scale to measure use of evidence in evidence-based management
science.summary: Evidence-based management is increasingly used by organizations to aid in decision-making, but research in this area is limited. In a new study, researchers developed and validated a new measure—the Evidence-Based Management Source Utilization Scale (EBM-SUS)—that is tailored to management and that captures the extent to which decision makers draw on four sources of evidence.

science.title: Deep-rooted grass stores significantly more carbon, says new study
science.summary: Soil biologist Eric Slessarev has some advice for conservationists, landscapers, and farmers with fallow fields: Go touch deep-rooted grass. Or better yet, go plant some. Slessarev, an assistant professor of ecology and evolutionary biology in Yale's Faculty of Arts and Sciences, is the first author of a new study in Earth's Future showing that deep-rooted grasses store significantly more carbon in their root biomass than shallow-rooted crops—without harming the existing organic material already in the ground.

science.title: Photonic chip generates milliwatt-level UV light, 100 times brighter than before
science.summary: Researchers from the University of Twente and Harvard University have developed a new way to generate ultraviolet (UV) light on a photonic chip at power levels high enough for real-world use. For the first time, the technique produces milliwatt-level UV light on a chip. It is an important step for quantum technology, optical atomic clocks and advanced measurement equipment. The research is published in the journal Nature Communications.

science.title: Mapping the hidden structure of the universe
science.summary: The universe has a hidden structure, and a University of Virginia professor is mapping it in 3D, using 46 million galaxies and quasars and 19 million stars. Satya Gontcho A Gontcho, an assistant professor in the Department of Astronomy, is part of a team using the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory-led, Arizona-based Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument to conduct one of the most extensive surveys of the cosmos ever. DESI has built the largest 3D map of the universe ever created by humanity to study dark energy, one of the biggest mysteries in physics.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.summary: Lovitură dură pentru cultură! Probabil singura bibliotecă din țară unde românii încă se duceau cu plăcere, terasa „La Bibliotecă” din Cișmigiu, a fost dărmâmată de Poliția Locală. Nu e tocmai o surpriză că niște polițiști de mâna a doua, cum sunt cei de la Locală, dărâmă o bibliotecă, dar asta nu face gestul lor mai […] The post Cultura, în corzi! Singura bibliotecă unde mai mergea lumea, Biblioteca Cișmigiu, a fost dărâmată appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: UK agrees ban on cigarette sales for people born after 2008
mix_items.summary: Both chambers of Britain's parliament have approved a bill that would ban children aged 17 and under from buying cigarettes during their lifetime.

mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: How life-saving HIV therapy has contributed to increased rates of a sexually transmitted disease
mix_items.summary: A study in Health Economics uncovered an unintended consequence of a major medical breakthrough: while the availability of HIV treatments in the late 1990s dramatically improved survival, they also contributed to a resurgence in syphilis, a sexually transmitted infection.

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate
mix_items.summary: De ce vorbim despre bullying Bullyingul nu mai este de mult o problemă „minoră” sau o etapă inevitabilă a copilăriei.... Articolul Bullyingul în adolescență: răni invizibile și cum pot fi vindecate apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Why electroconvulsive therapy remains contested: Families report memory loss, worse quality of life and limited benefits
mix_items.summary: Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), which involves passing electricity through the brain under general anesthesia to cause a seizure, usually between six and 12 times, is used to varying degrees around the world for patients with depression and other mental conditions. A study in Psychology and Psychotherapy: Theory, Research and Practice reveals that the experiences of families and friends of individuals undergoing ECT have challenged claims that the therapy is safe and effective.

mix_items.title: Părinte Social
mix_items.summary: Descrierea postului: Părintele social crează un mediu de viaţă, îngrijire şi educaţie, caracterizat prin afecţiune şi stabilitate şi adecvat dezvoltării personalităţii fiecărui copil (în plan fizic, psihic, emoţional, social şi... The post Părinte Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.source: Hope and Homes for Children Romania
mix_items.title: „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj
mix_items.summary: Hope and Homes for Children, alături de Distribuție Oltenia, parte din Evryo, a derulat campania „Grijă pentru mine”, un proiect dedicat sănătății emoționale aadolescentelor din medii vulnerabile. Inițiativa a ajuns la peste 250 de fete din județul Gorj, inclusiv tinere din sistemul de protecție și din comunități rurale, oferindu-le sprijin pentru a-și înțelege emoțiile, a-șicrește... Articolul „Grijă pentru mine” – campanie dedicată adolescentelor din Gorj apare prima dată în Hope and Homes for Children .

mix_items.summary: London’s newest major museum, V&A East, launches in Olympic Park The post V&A East opens as ‘civic space’ for creativity and community appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: De la un proiect Teach for Romania la o inițiativă europeană: cum elevii din Reghin construiesc „Leadership HUB”
mix_items.summary: O idee pornită din dorința de a dezvolta competențele de leadership ale elevilor s-a transformat, în doar câțiva ani, într-o inițiativă educațională care conectează școli din mai multe țări europene. Povestea proiectului „Leadership HUB” începe cu un proiect finanțat de Teach for Romania și continuă astăzi prin colaborări internaționale în cadrul platformei eTwinning și al […]

mix_items.title: Does stress during pregnancy affect offspring's metabolic health?
mix_items.summary: Physical activity during pregnancy enhances the metabolic health of offspring, but new research in mice indicates that prenatal stress blunts these benefits, at least in male offspring. The findings are published in The FASEB Journal.

mix_items.title: How children in rural and urban areas experience different mental health struggles
mix_items.summary: A study in Pediatric Investigation found that rural and urban children and adolescents tend to experience different mental health issues, with those living in the country showing more emotional problems and those in the city demonstrating more behavioral issues.

mix_items.title: Do drugs that similarly combat a disease have differing effects on quality of life?
mix_items.summary: A study published in Rheumatology & Autoimmunity challenges the assumption that achieving clinical remission in rheumatoid arthritis is sufficient, showing that patient-reported outcomes vary significantly by drug class even when disease activity is comparable.

mix_items.title: Study finds prostate cancer overdiagnosis rises sharply with age
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Queen Mary University of London have found that the likelihood of prostate cancer overdiagnosis—the detection of a cancer that would never have been diagnosed during a patient's lifetime but for PSA screening—is low in younger men but rises substantially with old age.

mix_items.title: Cell membranes may store memories after electrical stimulation
mix_items.summary: The science of memories has been pursued and studied since the days of ancient Greece and Aristotle. Today, research conducted by Dima Bolmatov, assistant professor in the Department of Physics & Astronomy at Texas Tech University, is considering how memories are stored on a cellular level.

mix_items.title: Collagen analysis finds wider prey use by Neanderthals and modern humans
mix_items.summary: The Centro Nacional de Investigación sobre la Evolución Humana (CENIEH) has taken part in a study published in the International Journal of Osteoarchaeology that provides new insights into subsistence strategies during the Middle to Upper Paleolithic at the site of Cova Eirós (Cancelo, Triacastela, Lugo). The research applies a collagen-based approach to improve the taxonomic identification of faunal remains.

mix_items.title: Some cancer drugs disrupt taste by changing the cells inside taste buds, study shows
mix_items.summary: Researchers at the University of Colorado Anschutz may have identified why many cancer patients say food suddenly tastes unpleasant during treatment. The study, published today in Development, found that a class of targeted cancer drugs known as tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs) can change how taste buds are maintained—reducing the ability to taste sweet foods and altering flavor perception overall. While the study was conducted in animal models, researchers believe similar changes likely occur in humans.

mix_items.title: Rose pangenome maps 55,000 genes, opening new path for breeding
mix_items.summary: Roses are among the most economically significant ornamental plants worldwide, with widespread applications in the cut flowers, garden, and cosmetics industries. Yet fewer than 10% of rose species have contributed to modern cultivated roses. Until recently, available technologies did not allow to fully sequence the Rosa subgenus.

mix_items.title: Researchers develop, validate new scale to measure use of evidence in evidence-based management
mix_items.summary: Evidence-based management is increasingly used by organizations to aid in decision-making, but research in this area is limited. In a new study, researchers developed and validated a new measure—the Evidence-Based Management Source Utilization Scale (EBM-SUS)—that is tailored to management and that captures the extent to which decision makers draw on four sources of evidence.

mix_items.title: Deep-rooted grass stores significantly more carbon, says new study
mix_items.summary: Soil biologist Eric Slessarev has some advice for conservationists, landscapers, and farmers with fallow fields: Go touch deep-rooted grass. Or better yet, go plant some. Slessarev, an assistant professor of ecology and evolutionary biology in Yale's Faculty of Arts and Sciences, is the first author of a new study in Earth's Future showing that deep-rooted grasses store significantly more carbon in their root biomass than shallow-rooted crops—without harming the existing organic material already in the ground.

mix_items.title: Photonic chip generates milliwatt-level UV light, 100 times brighter than before
mix_items.summary: Researchers from the University of Twente and Harvard University have developed a new way to generate ultraviolet (UV) light on a photonic chip at power levels high enough for real-world use. For the first time, the technique produces milliwatt-level UV light on a chip. It is an important step for quantum technology, optical atomic clocks and advanced measurement equipment. The research is published in the journal Nature Communications.

mix_items.title: Mapping the hidden structure of the universe
mix_items.summary: The universe has a hidden structure, and a University of Virginia professor is mapping it in 3D, using 46 million galaxies and quasars and 19 million stars. Satya Gontcho A Gontcho, an assistant professor in the Department of Astronomy, is part of a team using the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory-led, Arizona-based Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument to conduct one of the most extensive surveys of the cosmos ever. DESI has built the largest 3D map of the universe ever created by humanity to study dark energy, one of the biggest mysteries in physics.

mix_items.title: Can sparkling water boost metabolism and help with weight loss?
mix_items.summary: Sparkling water is often seen as a simple, healthy drink—but could it also help with weight loss? New research suggests it may slightly boost how the body processes blood sugar and energy. However, the effect is very small, meaning it’s no substitute for diet and exercise.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Sunt intr-o relatie serioasa cu butonul de snooze.
